---
title: "Exercise-05"
format: html
date: 3-4-36
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r} 


library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(fishualize)
library(mosaic)

ds<-"http://raw.githubusercontent.com/difiore/ada-datasets/main/IMDB-movies.csv"

movie<-read.csv(ds)

#step 2
filter_movies<-movie%>%
  filter(startYear >= 1920, startYear <= 1979)%>%
  filter(runtimeMinutes>=60, runtimeMinutes<=180)%>%
  mutate(decade = case_when(
    startYear >= 1920 & startYear <= 1929 ~ "1920s",
    startYear >= 1930 & startYear <= 1939 ~ "1930s",
    startYear >= 1940 & startYear <= 1949 ~ "1940s",
    startYear >= 1950 & startYear <= 1959 ~ "1950s",
    startYear >= 1960 & startYear <= 1969 ~ "1960s",
    startYear >= 1970 & startYear <= 1979 ~ "1970s",
  ))

#step 3
movie_time<-ggplot(data = filter_movies, aes(x = runtimeMinutes))+
  geom_histogram(fill = "cyan3", col = "cyan3")+
  facet_wrap(~ decade)+
  theme_classic()

#step 4 use a one-line statement to calculate the population mean and population standard deviation in runtimeminutes for each decade and save the results in a new dataframe called results 

results<- filter_movies%>%
  group_by(decade)%>%
  summarise(avg = mean(runtimeMinutes),
            sd = sd(runtimeMinutes),
            se = sd/sqrt(nrow(movie_decade)))

##step 5 draw a single sample of 100 movies, without replacement, from each decade and calculate the single sample mean and single sample standard deviation in runtimeminutes for each decade. 

reps<-100

movie_decade<-filter_movies%>%
  group_by(decade)%>%
  sample_n(size = 100, replace = FALSE)

mean_sd_movie<-movie_decade%>%
  group_by(decade)%>%
  summarise(avg = mean(runtimeMinutes),
            sd = sd(runtimeMinutes))

##step 6 calculate for each decade the standard error around your estimate of the population mean runtimeminutes based on the standard dev and sample size (n=100 movies) of your single sample.

mean_sd_movie<-movie_decade%>%
  group_by(decade)%>%
  summarise(avg = mean(runtimeMinutes),
            sd = sd(runtimeMinutes),
            se = sd/sqrt(nrow(movie_decade)))

##step 7 compare the estimates to the actual population mean runtimeMinutes for each decade and to the expected SE in the population mean for samples of size 100 based on the known population SD for each decade. 

joined<-full_join(results, mean_sd_movie, by = "decade") ##the means and standard deviations for the known population are very close to the means and standard deviation of the samples.

#step 8 Generate a sampling distribution of mean runtimeMinutes for each decade by [a] drawing 1000 random samples of 100 movies from each decade, without replacement, and, for each sample, [b] calculating the mean runtimeMinutes and the standard deviation in runtimeMinutes for each decade.


samp_dist<- do(1000)*sample_n(group_by(filter_movies, decade), size = 100, replace = FALSE)

##step 9 Then, calculate the mean and the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of sample means for each decade and plot a histogram of the sampling distribution for each decade. 

samp_dist_mean_sd<-samp_dist%>%
  group_by(decade)%>%
  summarise(avg = mean(runtimeMinutes),
            sd = sd(runtimeMinutes))

movie_time_his<-ggplot(data = samp_dist, aes(x = runtimeMinutes))+
  geom_histogram(fill = "cyan3", col = "cyan3")+
  facet_wrap(~ decade)+
  theme_classic()
#all the decades are skewed somewhat to the left, however the earlier decades are more dramatically skewed and the 50s, 60s, and 70s are starting to look more like a normal distribution. 

##step 10 compare the standard error in runtimeMinutes for samples of size 100 from each decade as estimted from your first sample of 100 movies, as calculated from the known population standar deviations for each decade 

samp_dist_mean_sd<-samp_dist%>%
  group_by(decade)%>%
  summarise(avg = mean(runtimeMinutes),
            sd = sd(runtimeMinutes),
            se = sd/sqrt(nrow(movie_decade)))

stand_error<-full_join(joined, samp_dist_mean_sd, by = "decade")


se<- stand_error%>%
  select(decade, se.x, se.y, se)

##the standard errors are all very close from the first sample of 100 movies, from the known population and as estimated from the sampling distribution. 
```

You can add options to executable code like this 

```{r}

##challenge 2

#step 1

ds2<-"https://raw.githubusercontent.com/difiore/ada-datasets/main/zombies.csv"

z<-read.csv(ds2)

#step 2 calculate the population mean and standard deviation for each quantitative random variable in the data dataset

pop_mean_sd<-z%>%
  select(-id)%>%
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric),
                   list(mean = mean, sd = sd),
                   na.rm = TRUE))

#step 3 Use {ggplot} and make boxplots of each of these variables by gender.

zhboxplot<-ggplot(data = z, aes(y=height))+
  geom_boxplot()+
  facet_wrap(~gender)+
  theme_classic()

zwboxplot<-ggplot(data = z, aes(y=weight))+
  geom_boxplot()+
  facet_wrap(~gender)+
  theme_classic()

zaboxplot<-ggplot(data = z, aes(y=age))+
  geom_boxplot()+
  facet_wrap(~gender)+
  theme_classic()

zkboxplot<-ggplot(data = z, aes(y=zombies_killed))+
  geom_boxplot()+
  facet_wrap(~gender)+
  theme_classic()

zeboxplot<-ggplot(data = z, aes(y=years_of_education))+
  geom_boxplot()+
  facet_wrap(~gender)+
  theme_classic()

#step 4 Use {ggplot} and make scatterplots of height and weight in relation to age (i.e., use age as the x variable), using different colored points for males versus females. Do these variables seem to be related? In what way?

zhscatter<-ggplot(data = z, aes(x=age, y = height, color = gender))+
  geom_point()+
  theme_classic()

##it seems that there is a positive correlation between age and height 

zwscatter<-ggplot(data = z, aes(x=age, y = weight, color = gender))+
  geom_point()+
  theme_classic()

#It does not seem that there is much of a relationship between weight and age. 

##step 5 Using histograms and Q-Q plots, check whether each of the quantitative variables seem to be drawn from a normal distribution. Which seem to be and which do not?

histogram(z$height)
histogram(z$weight)
histogram(z$zombies_killed)
histogram(z$years_of_education)
histogram(z$age)

qqnorm(z$height, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable") ##seems to be drawn from a normal distribution
qqline(z$height, col = "gray")

qqnorm(z$weight, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable") #drawn from a normal distribution
qqline(z$weight, col = "gray")

qqnorm(z$zombies_killed, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable")#not drawn from a normal distribution 
qqline(z$zombies_killed, col = "gray")

qqnorm(z$years_of_education, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable")#not drawn from a normal distribution
qqline(z$years_of_education, col = "gray")

qqnorm(z$age, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable") #drawn from a normal distribuiton
qqline(z$age, col = "gray")


##step 6

#Now use the sample_n() or slice_sample() function from {dplyr} to sample ONE subset of 50 zombie apocalypse survivors (without replacement) from this population and calculate the mean and sample standard deviation for each variable. Also estimate the standard error for each variable based on this one sample and use that to construct a theoretical 95% confidence interval for each mean. 

samplez<-sample_n(z, size = 50, replace = FALSE)

sample_mean_sd<-samplez%>%
  select(-id)%>%
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric),
                   list(mean = mean, sd = sd),
                   na.rm = TRUE))

sezh <- sd(samplez$height)/sqrt(length(samplez$height))

sezw <- sd(samplez$weight)/sqrt(length(samplez$weight))

sezk <- sd(samplez$zombies_killed)/sqrt(length(samplez$zombies_killed))

seze <- sd(samplez$years_of_education)/sqrt(length(samplez$years_of_education))

seza<- sd(samplez$age)/sqrt(length(samplez$age))

##calculate the confidence interval for each mean
cizh <- sample_mean_sd$height_mean + qnorm(c(0.025, 0.975))* sezh

cizw <- sample_mean_sd$weight_mean + qnorm(c(0.025, 0.975))* sezw

cizk <- sample_mean_sd$zombies_killed_mean + qnorm(c(0.025, 0.975))* sezk

cize <- sample_mean_sd$years_of_education_mean + qnorm(c(0.025, 0.975))* seze

ciza <- sample_mean_sd$age_mean + qnorm(c(0.025, 0.975))* seza

##step 7 Then draw another 199 random samples of 50 zombie apocalypse survivors out of the population and calculate the mean for each of the these samples. Together with the first sample you drew out, you now have a set of 200 means for each variable (each of which is based on 50 observations), which constitutes a sampling distribution for each variable.

k <- 199  # number of samples
n <- 50  # size of each sample
s <- list()  # create a dummy variable to hold each sample
# dummy variable needs to be a list, not a vector, since each element will hold
# a vector of numbers

##mean for height
for (i in 1:k) {
    s[[i]] <- sample(z$height, size = n, replace = FALSE)
}

head(s)

means_zh <- vector(length = k)  # create a dummy variable to hold the mean of each sample

for (i in 1:k) { ##create a loop to calculate the mean of each sample and store results as a vector
    means_zh[[i]] <- mean(s[[i]])
}
head(means_zh)

mean(means_zh)
sd(means_zh)

##mean for weight
for (i in 1:k) {
    s[[i]] <- sample(z$weight, size = n, replace = FALSE)
}

head(s)

means_zw <- vector(length = k)  # create a dummy variable to hold the mean of each sample

for (i in 1:k) { ##create a loop to calculate the mean of each sample and store results as a vector
    means_zw[[i]] <- mean(s[[i]])
}
head(means_zw)

mean(means_zw)
sd(means_zw)

##mean for age
for (i in 1:k) {
    s[[i]] <- sample(z$age, size = n, replace = FALSE)
}

head(s)

means_za <- vector(length = k)  # create a dummy variable to hold the mean of each sample

for (i in 1:k) { ##create a loop to calculate the mean of each sample and store results as a vector
    means_za[[i]] <- mean(s[[i]])
}
head(means_za)

mean(means_za)
sd(means_za)

##mean for zombies_killed
for (i in 1:k) {
    s[[i]] <- sample(z$zombies_killed, size = n, replace = FALSE)
}

head(s)

means_zk <- vector(length = k)  # create a dummy variable to hold the mean of each sample

for (i in 1:k) { ##create a loop to calculate the mean of each sample and store results as a vector
    means_zk[[i]] <- mean(s[[i]])
}
head(means_zk)

mean(means_zk)
sd(means_zk)

##mean for education
for (i in 1:k) {
    s[[i]] <- sample(z$years_of_education, size = n, replace = FALSE)
}

head(s)

means_ze <- vector(length = k)  # create a dummy variable to hold the mean of each sample

for (i in 1:k) { ##create a loop to calculate the mean of each sample and store results as a vector
    means_ze[[i]] <- mean(s[[i]])
}
head(means_ze)

mean(means_ze)
sd(means_ze)

##the means are similar but the standard deviations of the one sample of fifty is much larger than the sampling distribution standard deviations.

##step 8 plot the sampling distributions for each variable mean. what do they look like? are they normally distributed?

histogram(means_zh) #normally distributed
histogram(means_zw) #normally distributed
histogram(means_za)
histogram(means_zk) #normally distributed
histogram(means_ze) #normally distributed


qqnorm(means_zh, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable") ##seems to be drawn from a normal distribution
qqline(means_zh, col = "gray")

qqnorm(means_zw, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable") ##seems to be drawn from a normal distribution
qqline(means_zw, col = "gray")

qqnorm(means_za, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable") ##seems to be drawn from a normal distribution
qqline(means_za, col = "gray")

qqnorm(means_zk, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable") ##seems to be drawn from a normal distribution
qqline(means_zk, col = "gray")

qqnorm(means_ze, main = "QQ Plot - Random Normal Variable") ##seems to be drawn from a normal distribution
qqline(means_ze, col = "gray")


##all the distributions are now normally distributed whereas zombies killed and years of education were originally drawn from a poisson distribution 

#step 9 construct a 95% confidence intervale for each mean directly from the sampling distribution of sample means using the central 95% that distribution. 
```

The `echo: false` option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).
